The interlocking shall fail to a safe state on loss of power.
The supplier should provide the test records in English.
The contractor may apply for a deviation permit, provided that safety is preserved.
Access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization.

João, thanks for making time. I want to close the conformance statement for Clause 7 today. Let's start with 7.1, it says the interlocking shall fail to a safe state and shall log every command. Are those two firm, or is there room?

They're firm. Both are written with shall, so each is a mandatory requirement, there's no room. We can only claim conformance once each one is met and demonstrated by the stated verification method, the type test.
Understood. Now 7.2, the test records in English. We can only deliver them in German this week. Does that break conformance?

No. That line uses should, so it's an optional requirement, recommended, not binding. You don't lose conformance, provided that the departure is recorded and justified in the safety case. Send them in German for now, log the reason, and we're fine.
Good. One more, we'd like to swap the axle counter for an equivalent unit. Is that allowed, and does it change anything?

You may propose an equivalent component, it's a permission, provided that it holds the same safety integrity level. But the fail-safe behavior is a mandatory clause, so if the swap touches it, work shall not proceed on that function until we approve a deviation permit in writing.
Fair. My test engineer also asked whether her team can get into the vital core to speed up the integration test.

That one I have to stop. Access to the vital core must not be granted without dual authorization, it's an absolute prohibition, whatever the pressure on the schedule. And notwithstanding the rest, the safety case must be approved before energization. So: 7.1 mandatory, 7.2 recommended, the swap permitted under a permit, and the vital core off limits. Shall I write it up that way?
That line uses shall, so it's a mandatory requirement, binding for conformance, not a suggestion.
This one is a should, recommended, so a justified departure doesn't break conformance.
You may propose an equivalent component, provided that it holds the same safety integrity level.
Work shall not proceed on that function until the deviation permit is approved in writing.

João, before you get on the clarification call with the German supplier, let me tell you how I want you to read this specification, because the whole conformance statement hangs on reading it correctly. The document looks like plain English, and that is the trap. In a standard, the modal verbs are not style, they are the contract. When a clause says the interlocking shall fail to a safe state, shall means mandatory. It is not a prediction about the future, the way will would be, and it is not a polite suggestion. It is a requirement you have to meet and demonstrate, or you cannot claim conformance. So every shall in that document is a line item you own. Now, should is the one people get wrong in both directions. Should is a recommendation, strongly advised good practice, but it is not binding. A supplier who departs from a should does not lose conformance, provided that the departure is recorded and justified. Do not read a should as if it were a shall and over-specify the contract, and do not read it as optional, therefore ignore it, because the intent still matters. Then there is may. May is permission, not obligation. When the clause says the contractor may propose an equivalent component, it means you are allowed to, not that you have to. And must, when you see it, is a necessity, usually a safety one. Must not is an absolute prohibition, full stop. So as you read, mark every clause: is this a shall, a should, a may, or a must not. Get that map right and the call is easy. Confirm the mandatory requirements, flag the recommendations, and if a change touches a shall, tell them a deviation permit is needed before any work proceeds.

Three rules for reading a specification, from someone who has audited a lot of conformance claims. First, the modal verb is the requirement, so read it before you read anything else. Engineers skim a clause for the technical noun and miss whether it says shall or should, and those two words decide whether the clause is binding. Underline the modal in every clause before you argue about the content. Shall and shall not are mandatory. Should and should not are recommended. May is permitted. Must and must not carry necessity and prohibition. If you cannot say which of those a clause is, you have not read it yet. Second, respect the scope of application. A requirement only binds inside the scope the clause names, and a cross-reference tells you which other clause you have to read with it. People quote a shall from a section that does not even apply to their equipment, or they read one clause alone when it points to an annex that changes it. Find the scope, follow the cross-reference, and only then decide what conforms. Third, know the two honest ways out of a requirement you cannot meet. Either the product is deemed to conform because it passed the stated verification method, the type test, the inspection, or the analysis the standard names, or, if it genuinely cannot meet a mandatory clause, you apply for a deviation permit and you do not proceed on that function until the permit is approved in writing. Conformance is not an opinion and it is not a negotiation. It is every shall met and demonstrated, every deviation documented, and nothing on the vital core changed without authorization. Read the modal, respect the scope, and prove it by the method the standard sets. That is the whole discipline.
